## [00:00 - 03:02]

The second chemical that's released is called oxytocin. And oxytocin is a very human, it's a very humanizing chemical. And when you share a story that evokes this, it makes people feel really deeply connected with you. So this is what happens when you share a story that evokes oxytocin. The first thing is that it increases the level of generosity that people feel towards you. It also increases bonding. It also increases empathy. And finally, it also increases levels of trust. These are the wonderful things that happen when you share a story that evokes oxytocin. Let's go into an example with this now. With oxytocin, before I dive into this next video, I never used to share stories that were more vulnerable in nature. And that's what you'll find with stories that evoke oxytocin. They tend to be more vulnerable stories. And I have to give credit where it's due because the vin that you see now wasn't the vin that was from five years ago. Because five years ago, I would never share a story that was even in the slightest bit vulnerable. However, once I came across the works of Bernay Brown, I became extremely inspired to start to share more of that vulnerability, especially with my students. So with this particular story, I just want you to know that it's still a hard story for me to tell today. But I wanted to share it with you because it really shows the power behind vulnerability. And when you share stories that evoke oxytocin. So here's a story that evokes oxytocin. How many of you here are close to your grandparents? Yeah, me too. I am. That's my grandma. I can't believe how small she is. I swear she just shrinks when she gets older. She is one of the most important people to me in my life. And at the age of 86, she was diagnosed with lymphoma cancer. And I remember this moment so vividly in my mind where we're in the doctor's practice, the oncologist. And the whole family was there. There was about 18 people in the room. And I was sitting there. I'm the oldest grandson. And I was sitting there with the eldest grandchild. And I was there with my mom, and then me, and all my aunties, uncles, cousins. And grandma was feeling stomach pains. And we were about to hear the result. And the doctor says, your grandmother has lymphoma cancer in her stomach. And at her age of 86, there's no point in doing chemotherapy or radiation therapy or

## [06:10 - 07:11]

especially when it comes to love. And my mom watches this with so much regret because she rarely told her mom she loved her.
